description: Trigger discrimination for the beginner's WHOLE-JOURNEY navigator (this skill) vs the requirements INTERVIEW itself (requirements-gathering-facilitator), the TEAM AI-SDLC policy (ai-sdlc-operating-model), WRITING the spec (product-spec-writer), and a SINGLE-STAGE request from someone who knows their next step (code-reviewer). This skill wins the meta-navigation framing ('where do I start / what's next / take me idea-to-shipped') and INVOKES the others as it routes; it must not steal their primary triggers.

cases.prompt: I'm not a developer. I have an idea for a product but I genuinely don't know where to start or what the steps are. Take me from idea to shipped.
cases.note: SEAM CASE (primary) — the beginner asking WHERE they are and WHAT COMES NEXT across the whole journey is this skill. It will invoke requirements-gathering-facilitator as stage 1, but the framing is navigate-the-journey, not run-the-interview.
cases.reason: Runtime stage detection + next-skill routing + plain-language translation for a non-developer who does not know the next step is this skill's core scope.

cases.prompt: Interview me with structured questions until we have a clear requirements brief for this feature — separate the real problem from my proposed solution.
cases.note: SEAM CASE (sharpest rival) — the user is asking for the discovery INTERVIEW itself, not for navigation across the lifecycle. That is the facilitator, which this skill delegates to.
cases.reason: Running the elicitation (users/jobs, problem-vs-solution, non-goals, conflict) is requirements-gathering-facilitator. project-orchestrator invokes it; it does not run the interview.

cases.prompt: Define how our engineering team and our AI agents should plan, implement, review, and merge work together — the stages, the gates, and who has authority at each.
cases.note: SEAM CASE — authoring the end-to-end operating model / authority map for a technical TEAM is ai-sdlc-operating-model. project-orchestrator drives ONE beginner's ONE project through that map and cites it, never authors it.
cases.reason: Writing the lifecycle policy (stages, gates, authority holders) is the operating model. This skill consumes that map to navigate a specific project, not to define team policy.

cases.prompt: Requirements are settled and signed off. Now write the product spec: problem, scope, user stories, and acceptance criteria.
cases.reason: With requirements known, authoring the specification is product-spec-writer. project-orchestrator would ROUTE to it at stage 2, but a direct 'write the spec' request from someone who knows their step goes straight to the owning skill.

cases.prompt: Review this diff and tell me what's wrong with it.
cases.reason: A single, well-scoped stage request from a user who already knows their next step is the owning stage skill. The whole-journey navigator is for the user who does NOT know the next step.
